aliases: Quartz (crystalline form, not used in food)

natural_occurrence.description: Silicon dioxide is one of the most abundant compounds on Earth, making up most sand and many rocks. Food-grade silica is amorphous (non-crystalline) form, not the crystalline quartz found in nature.

manufacturing.description: Food-grade amorphous silica is produced by synthetic processes (precipitated silica, fumed silica, silica gel) or by purifying natural diatomaceous earth. The amorphous form is essential - crystalline silica (quartz) is not used in food due to health concerns.

other_uses.cosmetics: Absorbent, abrasive, bulking agent, opacifier in cosmetics and toothpaste.
other_uses.industrial: Glass manufacturing, electronics, concrete, water filtration, insulation.
other_uses.pharmaceutical: Tablet and capsule diluent, glidant, anti-caking agent.
other_uses.household: Desiccant packets ('do not eat'), cat litter, toothpaste abrasive.

eu.max_level_mg_kg: quantum satis (10000 mg/kg in many categories)
eu.conditions: Only amorphous silica permitted. Maximum levels specified for certain food categories.

usa.notes: Widely used as anti-caking agent. Maximum 2% by weight in food.

safety_evaluations.jecfa_adi: not specified (acceptable for use at levels used)
safety_evaluations.efsa_adi: not specified (no safety concern for amorphous silica at current use levels)

regulatory_history.event: EFSA re-evaluated silicon dioxide and expressed need for more data on nanoparticles in food-grade silica, but confirmed safety at current use levels for conventional (non-nano) forms.

fun_facts: Literally SAND - but food-grade amorphous silica, not beach sand (which is crystalline quartz)
The most common anti-caking agent in TABLE SALT - check your salt container ingredients!
Those 'DO NOT EAT' silica gel packets in shoe boxes? Same compound, but in desiccant form
Completely inert and passes through the digestive system unchanged - not absorbed by the body
The second most abundant compound in Earth's crust after oxygen itself
Different from crystalline silica (quartz) which can cause silicosis when inhaled - food-grade is amorphous and safe
Has been used in food for decades with excellent safety record
